posts.title: Where you put context matters more than what's in it
posts.excerpt: A three-year, 50-model research review confirms position in the prompt changes the answer more than content does (the Lost-in-the-Middle geometry). Practical takeaway: put the most critical…
posts.author: Chris Harper
posts.content:
A three-year, 50-model research review confirms position in the prompt changes the answer more than content does (the Lost-in-the-Middle geometry). Practical takeaway: put the most critical instructions/evidence at the very start or end of the prompt, not buried in the middle.

---
*Sources: [LLM-Stats — The Position of Your Context Matters](https://llm-stats.com/blog/research/the-position-of-your-context-matters-for-llms)*

posts.title: Context engineering" is now the discipline; RAG didn't die, it grew up
posts.excerpt: RAG is being reframed as a "context engine" with intelligent retrieval at its core. For enterprise Q&A, combining vector retrieval with GraphRAG is reported to cover ~90% of needs, and adaptive…
posts.author: Chris Harper
posts.content:
RAG is being reframed as a "context engine" with intelligent retrieval at its core. For enterprise Q&A, combining vector retrieval with GraphRAG is reported to cover ~90% of needs, and adaptive patterns add cost discipline by varying retrieval depth with query complexity.

---
*Sources: [Meta-Intelligence — Context Engineering Guide](https://www.meta-intelligence.tech/en/insight-context-engineering) · [SmartScope — RAG vs MCP vs Skills](https://smartscope.blog/en/blog/context-engineering-overview/)*

posts.title: Keep prompts short; reasoning degrades fast
posts.excerpt: 2026 best-practice writeups note LLM reasoning quality starts dropping around ~3,000 tokens, with a practical sweet spot of 150–300 words for most tasks. The field is splitting cleanly into casual…
posts.author: Chris Harper
posts.content:
2026 best-practice writeups note LLM reasoning quality starts dropping around ~3,000 tokens, with a practical sweet spot of 150–300 words for most tasks. The field is splitting cleanly into casual prompting vs. production context engineering — treat them as different skills.

---
*Sources: [Thomas Wiegold — Prompt Engineering Best Practices 2026](https://thomas-wiegold.com/blog/prompt-engineering-best-practices-2026/)*

posts.title: Make evaluation a repeatable loop, not a vibe check
posts.excerpt: An evaluation-driven workflow — Define, Test, Diagnose, Fix — turns stochastic LLM output into an engineering loop, anchored by a "Minimum Viable Evaluation Suite" tiered for plain apps, RAG, and…
posts.author: Chris Harper
posts.content:
An evaluation-driven workflow — Define, Test, Diagnose, Fix — turns stochastic LLM output into an engineering loop, anchored by a "Minimum Viable Evaluation Suite" tiered for plain apps, RAG, and agentic tool use. Counterintuitive finding: "better" prompts can hurt without an eval set to catch regressions.

---
*Sources: [arXiv: When "Better" Prompts Hurt](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.22025) · [Empirical study of prompting techniques for SE tasks](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.05614)*

posts.title: NVIDIA launches Cosmos 3 — first fully open "omnimodel" for physical AI
posts.excerpt: (June 1, GTC Taipei) — A mixture-of-transformers model combining vision reasoning, world generation, and action prediction; open weights on Hugging Face with Super/Nano/Edge variants. Why it matters…
posts.author: Chris Harper
posts.content:
(June 1, GTC Taipei) — A mixture-of-transformers model combining vision reasoning, world generation, and action prediction; open weights on Hugging Face with Super/Nano/Edge variants. Why it matters for engineers: it's a genuinely open frontier model you can run and fine-tune, aimed at synthetic-data generation and robotics/AV policy work — cutting training/eval cycles from months to days.

---
*Sources: [NVIDIA Newsroom](https://nvidianews.nvidia.com/news/nvidia-launches-cosmos-3-the-open-frontier-foundation-model-for-physical-ai) · [Hugging Face](https://huggingface.co/blog/nvidia/cosmos-3-for-physical-ai)*

posts.title: Microsoft Build 2026: MAI models + Copilot becomes multi-model
posts.excerpt: (June 2–3) — Microsoft shipped its own MAI family, including MAI-Code-1-Flash (code generation) and MAI-Thinking-1 (reasoning, low token cost), and rebuilt Copilot to route work across OpenAI,…
posts.author: Chris Harper
posts.content:
(June 2–3) — Microsoft shipped its own MAI family, including MAI-Code-1-Flash (code generation) and MAI-Thinking-1 (reasoning, low token cost), and rebuilt Copilot to route work across OpenAI, Anthropic, and open-source models. The Windows Agent Framework hit production. Why it matters: cheaper first-party coding/reasoning models and explicit model-routing reduce lock-in and give devs more deployment options.

---
*Sources: [CNBC](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/06/02/microsoft-unveils-new-ai-models-lessen-reliance-on-openai-lower-costs.html) · [Tom's Guide live blog](https://www.tomsguide.com/news/live/microsoft-build-2026)*

posts.title: Open letter on synthetic-DNA tracking for AI bioweapon risk
posts.excerpt: (June 3) — Sam Altman, Dario Amodei, and other lab leaders signed a letter urging better tracking of synthetic DNA potentially designed by AI. Why it matters: a likely precursor to…
posts.author: Chris Harper
posts.content:
(June 3) — Sam Altman, Dario Amodei, and other lab leaders signed a letter urging better tracking of synthetic DNA potentially designed by AI. Why it matters: a likely precursor to compliance/screening requirements that could land on teams building in bio/health-adjacent AI.

---
*Sources: [Wired (via Techmeme)](https://www.wired.com/)*

posts.title: Anthropic Claude Opus 4.8 — strongest coding scores yet
posts.excerpt: (released May 28, rolling out this week) — 88.6% on SWE-bench Verified and 74.6% on Terminal-Bench 2.1, with parallel-subagent workflows and a 2.5x fast mode, all at the same $5/$25 per-1M pricing.…
posts.author: Chris Harper
posts.content:
(released May 28, rolling out this week) — 88.6% on SWE-bench Verified and 74.6% on Terminal-Bench 2.1, with parallel-subagent workflows and a 2.5x fast mode, all at the same $5/$25 per-1M pricing. Why it matters: top-tier agentic coding performance with no price increase — relevant if you're picking a default model for autonomous coding tasks.

---
*Sources: [LLM-Stats deep dive](https://llm-stats.com/blog/research/claude-opus-4-8-launch)*

posts.title: Specs Before Code: The "Waterfall in 15 Minutes" Workflow
posts.excerpt: Google engineer Addy Osmani's widely-shared LLM coding workflow guide emphasizes writing a spec.md collaboratively with the AI before writing any code, then generating a step-by-step plan from that…
posts.author: Chris Harper
posts.content:
Google engineer Addy Osmani's widely-shared LLM coding workflow guide emphasizes writing a `spec.md` collaboratively with the AI *before* writing any code, then generating a step-by-step plan from that spec. The key insight: planning first forces you and the AI onto the same page, prevents wasted cycles, and dramatically improves output quality. Other key tips include breaking work into small iterative chunks, committing frequently as "save points," maintaining `CLAUDE.md` rules files to steer the AI toward your team's style, and running AI-on-AI code reviews (e.g., write with Claude, review with Gemini).

---
*Sources: [Addy Osmani's Blog](https://addyosmani.com/blog/ai-coding-workflow/) · [Substack](https://addyo.substack.com/p/my-llm-coding-workflow-going-into)*

posts.title: Dual-Tool Strategy: Use Claude Code and Cursor for Different Jobs
posts.excerpt: Updated June 3, the most comprehensive 2026 coding agent comparison confirms that high-velocity teams use Cursor for inline editing and daily feature work, and Claude Code for autonomous multi-file…
posts.author: Chris Harper
posts.content:
Updated June 3, the most comprehensive 2026 coding agent comparison confirms that high-velocity teams use Cursor for inline editing and daily feature work, and Claude Code for autonomous multi-file tasks and architectural changes. Claude Code's new Dynamic Workflows feature (in research preview) splits large jobs across parallel subagents — useful for codebase-wide refactors. The guide was just updated to reflect Claude Opus 4.8 (May 28), Copilot's usage-based billing (June 1), and Windsurf's rebrand to Devin Desktop (June 2).

---
*Sources: [Cosmic.js Comparison Guide](https://www.cosmicjs.com/blog/claude-code-vs-github-copilot-vs-cursor-which-ai-coding-agent-should-you-use-2026) · [Codersera Full Guide](https://codersera.com/blog/ai-coding-agents-complete-guide-2026/)*
